coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for the facile and visual detecting Al3+ and the design of logic device
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Min
Zhao, Yourong

Gao, Hongmei

Ning, Zhanglei
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent probe (CDs@Eu-MOFs) was synthesized by modifying lanthanide metal–organic framework with carbon dots using a post-synthetic modification method. The composite exhibits strong red-green dual-emission fluorescence characteristics and demonstrates a significant fluorescence signal towards aluminum ions. These results indicate that the prepared CDs@Eu-MOFs materials can serve as selective fluorescent probes for detecting aluminum ions. The relative ratios of fluorescence intensity (I536/I616) exhibited a good linear relationship with Al3+ concentration within the range of 10−5–10−3 M with a calculated limit of detection at 0.306 μΜ. The change in fluorescence can be easily observed by the naked eye under a UV lamp. The potential detection mechanisms are discussed, indicating that Al3+ may interact with N atoms resulting in reduced fluorescence. Furthermore, an intelligent molecular logic gate was constructed based on the correlation between Al3+ and the luminescence response signals of the probes, enabling direct smart recognition of Al3+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Ratiometric detection
Al3+
Logic gate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of the reported La(C9H6O6)(H2O)6, the synthetic crystalline Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs samples; (b) FT-IR spectra of Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs; (c) XPS spectra of Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs samples; (d) Histogram of Zeta potential values of CDs, Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs samples.

(a) The excitation and emission spectra of Eu-MOFs; (b) excitation and emission spectra of CDs; (c) emission spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs samples at different excitation wavelengths; (d) emission spectrum of CDs@Eu-MOFs, illustrated by CIE chromaticity diagram of CDs@Eu-MOFs.

(a) The relative ratios of fluorescence intensity (I536/I616) of CDs@Eu-MOFs toward different metal ions; (b) The quenching rates of fluorescence intensity ratio (I536/I616) of CDs@Eu-MOFs for different metal ions; (c) Photographs of the fluorescence of CDs@Eu-MOFs containing different metal ions under UV light irradiation.

(a) Luminescence response of CDs@Eu-MOFs samples to Al3+ in the presence of other interfering ions; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs in response to different concentrations of Al3+; (c) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of CDs@Eu-MOFs samples and Al3+ concentration.

(a) Schematic of the operation of the fluorescence probe; (b) Histogram of normalized fluorescence intensity at 536 nm and 616 nm; (c) Schematic of the logic gates; (d) Truth table corresponding to the logic gates.
The performance of different probes to detect Al3+.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for the facile and visual detecting Al3+ and the design of logic device

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent probe (CDs@Eu-MOFs) was synthesized by modifying lanthanide metal–organic framework with carbon dots using a post-synthetic modification method. The composite exhibits strong red-green dual-emission fluorescence characteristics and demonstrates a significant fluorescence signal towards aluminum ions. These results indicate that the prepared CDs@Eu-MOFs materials can serve as selective fluorescent probes for detecting aluminum ions. The relative ratios of fluorescence intensity (I536/I616) exhibited a good linear relationship with Al3+ concentration within the range of 10−5–10−3 M with a calculated limit of detection at 0.306 μΜ. The change in fluorescence can be easily observed by the naked eye under a UV lamp. The potential detection mechanisms are discussed, indicating that Al3+ may interact with N atoms resulting in reduced fluorescence. Furthermore, an intelligent molecular logic gate was constructed based on the correlation between Al3+ and the luminescence response signals of the probes, enabling direct smart recognition of Al3+.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aluminum, naturally occurring in the trivalent state (Al3+) as oxides and inorganic salts, is one of the most abundant metals in the environment [1,2]. It is widely utilized across various fields such as textile manufacturing, construction industry, packaging materials, kitchen utensils, pharmaceuticals, and food additives [3,4]. Human exposure to Al3+ occurs intermittently through drinking water, medicine as well as food. However, excessive aluminum can have significant detrimental effects on both human life and environmental systems due to natural processes as well as by excessive human activities. It has been reported that a certain amount of Al3+ can impede the growth of plant roots and hinder the survival, reproduction and growth of fish and amphibians [5–7]. A more urgent concern is the potential harm to human health caused by the excessive intake of Al3+. Accumulation of Al3+ in the brain is associated with neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease [8], Parkinson's disease [3] and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis [9]. In addition, aluminum can also accumulate in the bones leading to diseases as osteochondrosis and osteodystrophy [10]. Nowadays, traditional detection techniques are employed for detection Al3+, including electrochemical methods [11], tripping voltammetry [12], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [13] and electrochemical method [14]. However, these techniques require expensive instruments, intricate sample pre-treatment and cumbersome operational procedures which greatly restrict their applicability in the field settings or real-time scenarios [15]. Therefore, the development of a convenient, cost-effective and highly sensitive method for aluminum detection holds significance practical importance.

Fluorescence sensing technology has attracted considerable attention in the field of chemical analysis and sensing field due to its inherent advantages such as low detection cost, user-friendly operation, high sensitivity, and visualized detection capabilities [16–19]. Several fluorescent materials have been utilized for detecting Al3+ ions, including small organic molecules, nanoparticles, and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [20–22]. However, the Al3+ detection remains a major challenge due to the limited spectral characteristics, restricted coordination capability, and pronounced hydration ability of Al3+ [23,24]. The reported Al3+ probe presents synthetic challenges and poor water solubility [25]. Moreover, most of these fluorescent probes emits only a single emitted signal, which is susceptible to various confounding factors such as light source fluctuation, instrumental interference, and probe concentration interference. The ratio fluorescence method is used for target measurement by assessing the fluorescence intensity ratio at two distinct wavelengths as the signal parameter [26]. This approach particularly emphasizes the demand for non-toxic ratiometric fluorescent probes, which are highly appealing and remain urgently required.

Logic gates are an essential component of electronic and digital circuits, carrying out Boolean logic operations to convert input signals into special output signals [27]. The systems that perform logic operations at the molecular level are referred to as molecular logic gates. In molecular logic gates, molecules such as metal ions, anions, DNA, enzymes, etc., are considered as input stimuli and then converted into output signals (UV absorption, fluorescence emission, circular dichroism, etc.) by the logic system [28]. These molecular logic devices exhibit significant potential for applications in chemical sensing, biomedicine, and diagnostics [29]. In the field of fluorescence sensing, probe typically respond to various factors, which facilitates their integration with logic gates. Our previous work has successfully combined a sensing probe with molecular logic gates for intelligent detection of styrene, highlighting the immense promise of fluorescence sensing in logic devices [30].

Hererin, we attempted to integrate Ln-MOFs with CDs to design dual-emission fluorescent composites (CDs@Eu-MOFs) for Al3+ sensing. This novel fluorescent probe was successfully synthesized using a post-synthetic modification method. Extensive tests and analyses were conducted on the luminescence performance of the composite material, which exhibited a strong fluorescence signal for Al3+ in water. Moreover, it exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Al3+. The fluorescence of CDs@Eu-MOFs material showed a significant color change under UV lamp after the addition of Al3+, indicating that its potential use as a new fluorescent probe for Al3+ detection.

The reagents used in the experiments were purchased directly from the market and used without further purification. Ultrapure water was manufactured by our laboratory. The characterization equipment used in the experiments is consistent with our previously reported work.

Eu-MOFs were synthesized using a room temperature precipitation method. Firstly, 0.1051 g (0.5 mmol) of trimesic acid was dissolved in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Then, 10 mL of 0.05 M aqueous Eu(NO3)3·6H2O was added to the mixture and the reaction was stirred for 1 h. After centrifugation and washing, the resulting white precipitate was collected. Finally, the samples were dried at 70 °C in an oven.

CDs were synthesized according to the reported literature [31]. A total of 0.8 g of o-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. Then it was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene lined high-pressure reactor. The reaction took place 200 °C under vacuum oven for 3 h to obtain a dark yellow solution. After cooling, the unreacted macromolecules were removed by filtration using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The solution was further purified by dialysis through a molecular weight 3500 Da dialysis bag in anhydrous ethanol for 24 h to obtain a bright yellow carbon dots solution.

Carbon dots functionalized Eu-MOFs (CDs@Eu-MOFs) were synthesized by a post-synthetic modification method [32]. 100 mg sample of the synthesized Eu-MOFs was immersed in 10 mL of carbon dots solution, and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the resulting composite CDs@Eu-MOFs were obtained by centrifugation and subsequently dried at 70 °C.

A total of 3 mg of the sample was added to 5 mL of ethanol for ultrasonic treatment to form Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs dispersions. Subsequently, fluorescence spectra were measured. The concentration of metal ion solution used was 10−3 M, with all cations (Mx+=Al3+, Co2+, Na+, Li+, K+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Pd2+) present in the form of nitrate. 1 mL metal ion solution was introduced into dispersion containing CDs@Eu-MOFs followed by ultrasound treatment. Fluorescence spectra were then measured for analysis.

The crystal structures of the synthesized samples were firstly analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the diffraction peaks of the synthesized Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs samples are consistent with the locations of the diffraction peaks of La(C9H6O6)(H2O)6, indicating that both sets of samples have the same crystal configuration as the simulated single crystals. The slight high-angle shift in the diffraction peak of the Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs samples can be attributed to differences in rare earth ions radii according to the Bragg's Law
( 2 d s i n θ = n λ )
[33]. In addition, the absence of the remaining heterogeneous peaks in the XRD patterns of CDs@Eu-MOFs indicates that the doped CDs would not destroy the structure of the materials. The infrared spectra (FT-IR) of the CDs@Eu-MOFs and Eu-MOFs are shown in Fig. 1b to further investigate the chemical structure of Eu-MOFs with CDs@Eu-MOFs. Bands at 1614, 1558, 1435, and 1374 cm−1 in the infrared spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of –COO−. The band at 530 cm−1 indicates the Eu-O stretching vibration, possibly from the free carboxylate ions. These results confirm successful coordination of Eu3+ to the carboxyl group. Furthermore, the peak at 3409 cm−1 is attributed to hydroxyl stretching vibration of water, which act as a solvent and participate in the coordination during the synthesis.

The morphologic structures of Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs are depicted in Fig. S1(a, c), revealing straw-like structures in both samples. This observation demonstrates that the introduction of CDs does not significantly affect the morphologic structure, which is consistent with the XRD results. Furthermore, energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) was employed to investigate changes in elemental composition within the composite material. A comparison between Eu-MOFs (Fig. S1b) and CDs@Eu-MOFs (Fig. S1d) reveals that only C, O, and Eu elements are present in Eu-MOFs sample, while additional N elements are detected in CDs@Eu-MOFs along with C, O, and Eu elements. The presence of N element can be attributed to the doped carbon quantum dots, confirming successful incorporation of CDs into Eu-MOFs. In the elemental mapping of the CDs@Eu-MOFs material shown in Fig. S2, it is observed that four elements, namely C, O, Eu and N, are uniformly dispersed within the MOFs matrix material. This observation in agreement with the EDX results. The XPS of the Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs samples are shown in Fig. 1c. The exclusive presence of the N 1S peak in the XPS energy spectrum of CDs@Eu-MOFs further corroborates the EDX result. It can be speculated that hydrogen bonding interactions may play a role in facilitating the binding between CDs and Eu-MOFs due to –NH2 groups on the surface of prepared CDs material and –COOH groups present within Eu-MOFs structure [34]. The zeta potential data (Fig. 1d) indicates that both CDs and Eu-MOFs have negatively charged surface potentials. Upon introducing negatively charged CDs into negatively charged Eu-MOFs, the negative charge of the composite CDs@Eu-MOFs increases according to zeta potential principle [35]. Furthermore, thermogravimetric tests (Fig. S3) were conducted to analyze the thermal stability of the materials. It can be observed that both Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs display similar weight loss process, with an initial weight loss occurring within the temperature range of 55 ∼ 125 °C due to crystalline water loss. No additional weight loss was observed below 450 °C, while a second phase of weight loss occurred between 450 ∼ 525 °C, attributed to the ligand decomposition. These results indicate that both Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs materials possess excellent thermal stability properties.

Luminescence spectra of the prepared CDs, Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs were obtained at room temperature. The photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of Eu-MOFs samples are given in Fig. 2 a. The excitation spectrum of the Eu-MOFs sample exhibited a broad peak within the range of 200 ∼ 350 nm, with a maximum excitation wavelength at 262 nm. This broad peak is attributed to the photoexcitation process involving electron transitions between Eu3+ ions and ligands [36,37]. The emission spectrum of Eu-MOFs was obtained at 262 nm, revealing six distinct emission peaks at 536, 556, 591, 616, 650 and 691 nm corresponding to the specific energy levels transitions of 5D1 → 7F1 and 5D0 → 7FJ (J = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4) [38,39]. Notably, the strongest emission peak of Eu-MOFs was located at 616 nm. The corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram coordinates was (0.488, 0.304) (Fig. S4a), confirming its typical red-emitting nature. The excitation and emission peaks of CDs are shown in Fig. 2b. The maximum emission peak of CDs occurs at a wavelength of 536 nm with CIE chromaticity diagram coordinates located at (0.302, 0.648) (Fig. S4b), showing their green-emitting fluorescent properties.

The excitation and emission spectra of composite CDs@Eu-MOFs were investigated. The excitation spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs were firstly analyzed using 536 nm and 616 nm as monitoring wavelengths. From Fig. S5a, it can be clearly observed that at a monitoring wavelength of 616 nm, one of the strongest excitation peaks in the range of 200 ∼ 300 nm occurs at 262 nm, which ascribed to the absorption of the ligand. Subsequently, by monitoring a wavelength of 536 nm (Fig. S5b), the strongest excitation peak with the range of 200 ∼ 400 nm was observed at 280 nm. The change in the strongest excitation peak suggests that the introduction of CDs may have an impact on the absorption of the material. Based on these results, an excitation wavelength range of 262 ∼ 282 nm was selected for further analysis. As shown in Fig. 2c, it can be observed that as the excitation wavelength increases, there is gradual decrease in characteristic emission intensity from Eu3+, while fluorescence emission intensity of CDs increases. Therefore, a wavelength of 272 nm was chosen for subsequent testing purposes. The emission spectra of the CDs@Eu-MOFs composites excited at 272 nm and their corresponding CIE chromaticity maps are shown in Fig. 2d. It can be seen that this material exhibits emission peaks attributed to both CDs and Eu-MOFs, thereby demonstrating red-green dual-emitting fluorescent nature of the CDs@Eu-MOFs material. Moreover, the CIE coordinate of this sample is (0.345, 0.457) within the yellow region, which aligns well with the trichromatic principle. The successful construction of dual-emitting materials implies that CDs@Eu-MOFs composites hold promise as ratiometric fluorescent probe.

To investigate the potential of the material as a metal ion probe, we initially evaluated the selectivity of the CDs@Eu-MOFs ratiometric fluorescent probe. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs were recorded upon addition of various metal ions. As shown in Fig. S6, distinct metal ions exhibited varying degrees of influence on the fluorescence emission of CDs@Eu-MOFs. Compared with Al3+, all other metal ions exhibited less influence on the fluorescence intensity. Significantly, upon introduction of Al3+, the characteristic emission peak belonging to CDs (λem = 536 nm) was observable quenching, while the characteristic emission peak of Eu3+ (λem = 616 nm) did not change significantly. The fluorescence intensity ratios (I536/I616) of CDs@Eu-MOFs after adding various metal ions were compared, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a. It can be noticed that the fluorescence intensity ratios of Co2+, Na+, and Li+ towards the materials exhibit a slight increase, while those of K+, Mg2+, Sr2+, and Ni2+ to CDs@Eu-MOFs remain essentially unchanged. The fluorescence intensity ratios of Ba2+, Zn2+, and Pb2+ are slightly decreased. In particular, after the interaction with Al3+, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I536/I616) of CDs@Eu-MOFs decreased significantly compared with the original solution, and the quenching efficiency of its fluorescence intensity ratio was as high as 80 % according to the calculation formula ((1-I/I0) × 100 %), indicating favorable selectivity for Al3+ (Fig. 3b). Under UV lamp irradiation, the sample solution after the addition of Al3+ shows only red light, confirming selective recognition of Al3+ by CDs@Eu-MOFs with the naked eye (Fig. 3c).

Moreover, anti-interference experiments were performed on the CDs@Eu-MOFs samples. As shown in Fig. 4 a, it is observed that Al3+ still exhibited a significant quenching response for CDs@Eu-MOFs in the presence of other metal ions, leading to a substantial decrease in its fluorescence intensity ratio. A series of fluorescence performance test experiments have demonstrated the excellent specific recognition ability of CDs@Eu-MOFs material for Al3+. Therefore, this composite material is expected to be utilized as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Al3+. The selective experimental results clearly indicate that CDs@Eu-MOFs have a good response to Al3+. Subsequently, to investigate the sensitivity of CDs@Eu-MOFs material towards Al3+, the correlation of the fluorescence intensity ratio of the probes to different concentrations of Al3+ was further investigated. As presented in Fig. 4b, varying concentrations of Al3+ (10−5 ∼ 10−3 M) were introduced to the CDs@Eu-MOFs samples. The characteristic emission intensity of CDs at 536 nm exhibited a gradual decrease with increasing Al3+ concentration, while the intensity of the Eu3+ emission peak at 616 nm remained relatively constant. Furthermore, a linear relationship was established between the fluorescence intensity ratio at 536 nm and 616 nm (I536/I616) and the concentration of the added Al3+ (Fig. 4c). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I536/I616) showed a good correlation dependence with Al3+ concentration. The fitted linear equation is I536/I616 = −0.1708 lg[C]-0.3706 with a high correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9915. The limit of detection (LOD) can be determined using the following two equations:
(1)
S b
=
∑
C 0
-
C 1
2
N - 1
(2)
LOD =
t
n-1,0.99
×
S b
Where Sb represents the standard deviation of the blank sample calculated by converting the measurement results into sample content (N = 21), C0 denotes the content value in the blank sample, and C1 is the mean value of C0; t(n-1,0.99) refers the t distribution at a degree of freedom of N-1 and confidence level of 99 %, and t being taken as 2.528 at N = 21. The limit of detection (LOD) of the material was calculated to be 0.306 μΜ. The detection limit is significantly lower than the health standard level of aluminum in drinking water (0.2 mg/L, 7.4 μΜ) as proposed in the Sanitary Standard for Drinking Water (GB 5749–2022) issued by the State Administration of Market Administration of the People's Republic of China and the Al3+ tolerance limit set by the USA Environmental Protection Agency (7.4 μΜ) [1]. Compared with previously reported studies on Al3+ detection (Table 1 ), this study achieved lower LODs and a wider detection range with LODs below the specified maximum limits. The developed probe in this experiment demonstrates a simple, rapid, color change discernible to the naked eye and a strong response to Al3+, making it suitable for Al3+ detection purposes.

A series of experimental tests were conducted to investigate the possible sensing mechanism of CDs@Eu-MOFs materials for Al3+. As shown in Fig. S7, the powder X-ray diffractogram of the sample after the addition of Al3+ reveals identical diffraction peaks to those of the CDs@Eu-MOFs, indicating that the quenching of fluorescence is not attributed to structural disruption and demonstrating excellent stability [51,52]. Comparison between the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of metal ions and the excitation spectrum of CDs@Eu-MOFs rules out the possibility of internal filtration effect (IFE) mechanism-based quenching [53,54], as there is minimal overlap between them (Fig. S8). Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime is another crucial parameter to investigate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching [55,56]. Testing at 536 nm and 616 nm before and after adding Al3+ shows no significant change in fluorescence lifetime, thus excluding dynamic fluorescence quenching as a possible mechanism (Fig. S9) [57].

The CDs@Eu-MOFs material was analyzed using XPS to further investigate its intrinsic mechanism as a fluorescent probe for Al3+ detection [58]. The full XPS spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs and Al3+/CDs@Eu-MOFs are shown in Fig. S10a. Comparing the XPS spectra before and after the identification of Al3+, it can be found that a new peak belonging to Al 2p appears at 75.06 eV, distinct from the characteristic elemental orbital valence states such as Eu 3d, O 1s and C 1s. This observation indicates that Al3+ is bound to CDs@Eu-MOFs [42]. Subsequently, the N 1s peaks in the CDs@Eu-MOFs and Al3+/CDs@Eu-MOFs samples were analyzed for changes in orbital valence states and potential interaction sites. The N 1s peaks of CDs@Eu-MOFs are found at binding energies of 407.41 eV and 399.94 eV, while after complexation with Al3+, the binding energy of N 1s in the Al3+/CDs@Eu-MOFs samples significantly shifts to 407.93 eV and 400.31 eV. The energy shift is 0.52 eV and 0.37 eV (Fig. S10b), respectively. This result in a decrease in N atom density and indicates an interaction between Al3+ and N atoms leading to the fluorescence quenching of the material at a wavelength of 536 nm [40].

Based on the distinct fluorescence behaviors of CDs@Eu-MOFs materials (Fig. 5 a), we employed a molecular logic gate strategy for Al3+ identification. Ultraviolet light (UV) and Al3+ were designated as input signals, and the normalized fluorescence intensities at 536 nm and 616 nm serving as output signals using a threshold value of 0.5 (Fig. 5b). The operational design of the logic gate is shown in Fig. 5c, which consists of two logical operations, INHIBIT and YES, to form a complex combinational logic gate. The presence and absence of two input cells can be determined as binary numbers “1” and “0” respectively. The output unit normalizes the fluorescence intensity and compares it with the threshold value of 0.5, if it is higher than 0.5, the output is the binary number “1” and the fluorescence is “ON”; on the contrary, whereas if it is below this threshold, the output will be “0” and the fluorescence will be “OFF”. Fig. 5d provides the truth table corresponding to the logic gates in this study, demonstrating their behavior with four input combinations. When the input signal is (0, 0) or (1, 0), both INHIBIT and YES logic gate outputs are (0, 0). While the input signal is (1, 0), the logic gate outputs are (1, 1), resulting in fluorescence turn-on states at 536 nm and 616 nm. When the input signal is (1, 1), the output is (0, 1), indicating that the presence of Al3+ leads to fluorescence quenching at 536 nm. These above results exhibit that the designed smart logic device can be directly utilized to observe the fluorescence response behavior of the system.

In summary, a novel fluorescent probe (CDs@Eu-MOFs) fluorescent material with red-green dual-emission characteristics was designed and prepared. The CDs@Eu-MOFs were used as a fluorescent probe exhibit high sensitivity, high selectivity and excellent anti-interference performance for the detection of aluminum ions. The relative ratios of fluorescence intensity (I536/I616) exhibit a good linear relationship with Al3+ concentration. The response range was 10−5-10−3 M, with the detection limit of 0.306 μΜ. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color change exhibited by the CDs@Eu-MOFs probe can be easily monitored by the naked eye under UV light. The potential detection mechanisms can be attributed to the coordination interactions between Al3+ and the nitrogen atoms of the carbon dots in the MOFs structure. Furthermore, an intelligent molecular logic gate was constructed for the identification of Al3+, exhibiting great potential for safeguarding public health and overseeing the safety of food and the environment.

Min Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Yourong Zhao: Investigation, Data curation. Yuhong Xie: Methodology, Investigation. Hongmei Gao: Investigation. Wenwei Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Jie Che: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jing Zhou: Resources, Investigation. Hongda Li: Resources, Investigation. Daojiang Gao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhanglei Ning: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Project of Science & Technology Department of Sichuan Province (2021YFG0277), Sichuan Provincial Engineering Laboratory of Livestock Manure Treatment and Recycling (202306) and Sichuan Environmental Protection Key Laboratory of Persistent Pollutant Wastewater Treatment (PPWT2023-01).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A molecular rotor based ratiometric detection scheme for aluminium ions in water
J. Photochem. Photobio, A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.P. Pandey, A.M. Desai, P.K. Singh, A molecular rotor based ratiometric detection scheme for aluminium ions in water, J. Photochem. Photobio, A. 433 (2022) 114145.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive ratiometric fluorescence method for visual detection of aluminum ion based on chelation-enhanced photoluminescence
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Gao, C. Deng, J. Xiong, P. Zhu, Q. Chen, K. Tan, A sensitive ratiometric fluorescence method for visual detection of aluminum ion based on chelation-enhanced photoluminescence, Microchem. J. 150 (2019) 104096.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aluminium toxicosis: a review of toxic actions and effects
Interdiscip. Toxicol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.O. Igbokwe, E. Igwenagu, N.A. Igbokwe, Aluminium toxicosis: a review of toxic actions and effects, Interdisciplinary Toxicology. 12 (2019) 45-70.
